evidence: The module header states 'This is an "eventually" formulation of the Second Hardy–Littlewood conjecture' and erdosproblems.com/855 identifies the problem as the second Hardy-Littlewood conjecture; the decl states the eventual form pi(x+y) <= pi(x)+pi(y).
The statement is an inequality pi(x+y) <= pi(x) + pi(y) about the prime-counting function pi.
The problem ('weak sunflower problem' per erdosproblems.com/857) asks for the minimal family size forcing a k-sunflower (IsSunflower) among subsets of [n].
The conjecture asserts the density d_t of DivisorSumSet t (via Set.HasDensity) is asymptotically c1/log(t)^c2.
DivisorSumSet t is the set of n such that t is a sum of distinct divisors of n (Nat.divisors).
Erdos's proved weaker version bounds the density d_t of DivisorSumSet t between 1/log(t)^c3 and 1/log(t)^c4.
Asks whether an additive basis of order 2 with representation function tending to infinity must contain a minimal additive basis of order 2 (IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder).
Asks whether f(n) > epsilon log n forces an additive basis of order 2 to contain a minimal additive basis; Larsen and Larsen constructed a counterexample.
Erdos and Nathanson proved the minimal-additive-basis containment when f(n) > (log 4/3)^-1 log n for all large n.
Hartter and Nathanson proved there exist additive bases which do not contain any minimal additive bases.
Erdos-Nathanson question whether an additive basis of order 2 with 1_A*1_A(n) -> infinity can be partitioned into two disjoint additive bases of order 2; disproved (Lean) by Larsen.
The forum variant asks whether every maximal primitive subset of {2,...,n} has at least pi(n) elements, the size of the prime maximal primitive set.
F(A,X,k) counts indices i with [a_i,...,a_{i+k-1}] < X where the bracket is the least common multiple (Finset.range k).lcm.
Asks whether removing a suitable infinite subset from a minimal additive basis of order k leaves an additive basis of order k+1 (IsAsymptoticAddBasisOfOrder).
